questions.question: Explain the fundamental concept of thresholding in image processing. What is its primary purpose, and what is the key assumption made about the image histogram for effective global thresholding?
keyPoints: Thresholding is an image segmentation technique that classifies pixels into object or background based on their intensity value.
Its primary purpose is to create a binary image from a grayscale image by separating the foreground objects from the background.
A key assumption for effective global thresholding is that the image histogram is bimodal, showing two distinct peaks representing the foreground and background pixel intensities.

questions.question: Briefly describe the basic, iterative algorithm used for global thresholding. What is the typical initial estimate for the threshold value (T) in this algorithm?
keyPoints: The algorithm starts by selecting an initial threshold T (often the mean of the image's intensity values).
It then segments the image using T, creating two groups of pixels: G1 (pixels > T) and G2 (pixels <= T).
It calculates the new threshold T_new as the average of the mean intensities of G1 and G2.
It iterates this process until the change in T between iterations is below a predefined value, converging on the final threshold.

questions.question: Identify one major advantage and one significant limitation of using a simple global thresholding technique.
keyPoints: Advantage: It is computationally simple and fast to apply, as it uses a single threshold value for the entire image.
Limitation: It fails drastically when the image has non-uniform illumination or shadows, as a single threshold cannot account for varying background intensity levels across the image.
